topics.title: Heaps & Priority Queues
topics.story: Imagine an emergency room: patients keep arriving, but instead of being seen in order of arrival, the most critical patient always goes first. That's a heap — a partially ordered tree where the most important element is always at the top, instantly accessible in O(1), and where insert/extract costs only O(log n).
topics.analogy: A heap is like a tournament bracket where the champion is always the root. When the champion leaves, the runner-up bubbles up. When a new player arrives, they bubble up to find their rightful place.
topics.memoryAnchor: MIN-HEAP: parent ≤ children, root = minimum. MAX-HEAP: parent ≥ children, root = maximum. Java PriorityQueue is MIN-HEAP by default.
topics.firstPrinciples: A binary heap is a complete binary tree stored in an array. Parent of i = (i-1)/2. Children of i = 2i+1 and 2i+2. Heap property: parent ≤ children (min) or parent ≥ children (max). Insert: add at end, bubble up O(log n). Extract: remove root, swap with last, bubble down O(log n). Peek: O(1).
topics.bruteForce: For Kth largest: sort in O(n log n), return arr[n-k]. For running median: maintain sorted list, O(n) insertion each time → O(n²) total. For merge K sorted: brute merge all then sort O(total log total).
topics.optimizedApproach: Min-heap of size K for Top-K in O(n log K). Two heaps (max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half) for running median in O(n log n). Merge K sorted with a min-heap — always poll minimum, push next from same list — O(n log K).
topics.whenToUse: Top-K problems. Running median. Merge K sorted lists. Scheduling by priority. Dijkstra's shortest path. Kth smallest/largest in a stream. Any 'always pick the best available' problem.

examples.title: Kth Largest — Min-Heap of Size K
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"PriorityQueue<Integer> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();","variables":{"nums":"[3,2,1,5,6,4]","k":2},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Find 2nd largest. Min-heap will hold exactly 2 elements at all times."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"minHeap.offer(num); if size>k: poll","variables":{"num":3,"heap":"[3]"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Add 3. Size=1 ≤ k=2. Keep."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"minHeap.offer(num); if size>k: poll","variables":{"num":2,"heap":"[2,3]"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Add 2. Size=2 ≤ k=2. Keep. Top-2 so far: {2,3}."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"minHeap.offer(num); if size>k: poll","variables":{"num":1,"heap":"[2,3]","evicted":1},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Add 1. Size=3>2. Poll min(1). Heap: [2,3]. 1 can't be 2nd largest — correctly evicted."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"minHeap.offer(num); if size>k: poll","variables":{"num":5,"heap":"[3,5]","evicted":2},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Add 5. Size=3>2. Poll min(2). Heap: [3,5]. 2 can't be 2nd largest with 3 and 5 present."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"minHeap.offer(num); if size>k: poll","variables":{"num":6,"heap":"[5,6]","evicted":3},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Add 6. Poll 3. Heap: [5,6]. 3 can't be 2nd largest with 5 and 6."},{"line":11,"lineCode":"return minHeap.peek();","variables":{"heap":"[5,6]","result":5},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"heap.peek()=5. 5 is the 2nd largest (6 is 1st). Min-heap root=smallest of top-K=Kth largest. O(n log k) time."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["k=2, find 2nd largest"] --> B["Add 3: heap=[3]"]
    B --> C["Add 2: heap=[2,3]"]
    C --> D["Add 1: heap=[2,3] — evict 1"]
    D --> E["Add 5: heap=[3,5] — evict 2"]
    E --> F["Add 6: heap=[5,6] — evict 3"]
    F --> G["Add 4: heap=[5,6] — evict 4"]
    G --> H["peek()=5 = 2nd largest ✓"]
    style H fill:#d4edda
examples.explanation: Counterintuitive: use a MIN-heap for the K largest. The min-heap root is the smallest of the K largest elements — exactly the Kth largest. When size exceeds K, evict the minimum (it's now guaranteed not to be in the top K). O(n log k) time (each element causes at most one push and one poll, each O(log k)). O(k) space. Compare to sorting: O(n log n) time.
examples.realWorldUse: Top K frequent elements. K closest points to origin. Top K words in a document. Real-time leaderboard (top-K players by score). Recommendation systems (top-K items by relevance score). Twitter trending topics. Log analysis (top-K most frequent errors). The pattern works for any 'top-K' by any comparison criterion.

examples.title: Merge K Sorted Lists — Min-Heap
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":8,"lineCode":"for (ListNode node : lists) heap.offer(node);","variables":{"lists":"[1→4→5, 1→3→4, 2→6]","heap":"[1,1,2] (min-heap heads)"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Seed heap with heads: 1(list1), 1(list2), 2(list3). Heap has 3 nodes."},{"line":14,"lineCode":"ListNode node = heap.poll();","variables":{"polled":"1(list1)","heap":"[1,2]","tail.next":"1"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Poll min=1 (list1's head). Append to result. Push list1's next=4. Heap: [1,2,4]."},{"line":14,"lineCode":"ListNode node = heap.poll();","variables":{"polled":"1(list2)","heap":"[2,4]","result":"1→1"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Poll 1 (list2). Append. Push list2's next=3. Heap: [2,3,4]."},{"line":14,"lineCode":"ListNode node = heap.poll();","variables":{"polled":2,"heap":"[3,4]","result":"1→1→2"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Poll 2 (list3). Append. Push list3's next=6. Heap: [3,4,6]."},{"line":14,"lineCode":"continue polling...","variables":{"result":"1→1→2→3→4→4→5→6"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Continue until heap empty. Each poll is O(log k). Total n nodes polled → O(n log k) time."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["Heap: [1(L1),1(L2),2(L3)]"] --> B["Poll 1(L1), push 4"]
    B --> C["Heap: [1(L2),2(L3),4(L1)]"]
    C --> D["Poll 1(L2), push 3"]
    D --> E["Heap: [2(L3),3(L2),4(L1)]"]
    E --> F["Continue polling min, push next..."]
    F --> G["Result: 1→1→2→3→4→4→5→6 ✓"]
    style G fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION mergeKLists(lists):
  heap = MinHeap(comparator: node.val)

  // Seed: add head of each list
  FOR each list head: IF not null: heap.add(head)

  dummy = new Node; tail = dummy

  WHILE heap not empty:
    node = heap.poll()      // global minimum
    tail.next = node
    tail = node
    IF node.next != null:
      heap.add(node.next)   // bring in next from same list

  RETURN dummy.next
examples.explanation: The heap always holds exactly one node from each non-exhausted list — the current head (smallest unmerged element). Polling gives the global minimum across all K lists. Pushing the polled node's .next maintains the invariant. At most K nodes in the heap at any time. O(n log K) total where n = total nodes (each node polled once, heap of size K).
examples.realWorldUse: Database merge joins (K sorted partitions). Log aggregation (merge K time-sorted log files). External merge sort (RAM can't hold full data: read K chunks, merge with heap). Distributed sorting (K sorted shards). Merging K sorted result sets from parallel database queries.

examples.title: Running Median — Two Heaps
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"lower.offer(num); upper.offer(lower.poll());","variables":{"num":1,"lower":"[1]","upper":"[]"},"phase":"ADD","annotation":"Add 1: lower=[1]. Push lower.max(1) to upper. Upper=[1]. Upper(1)>lower(0): rebalance → lower=[1], upper=[]. median=1."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"lower.offer(num); upper.offer(lower.poll());","variables":{"num":2,"lower":"[1]","upper":"[2]"},"phase":"ADD","annotation":"Add 2: lower=[2,1]→push max(2)→upper=[2]. Equal sizes. median=(1+2)/2=1.5."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"lower.offer(num); upper.offer(lower.poll());","variables":{"num":3,"lower":"[2,1]","upper":"[3]"},"phase":"ADD","annotation":"Add 3: lower=[3,2,1]→push max(3)→upper=[3]. Upper(1)>lower(0) after: No, sizes 1 and 2, lower wins. median=lower.peek()=2."},{"line":14,"lineCode":"return lower.peek();","variables":{"lower":"[2,1]","upper":"[3]","median":2},"phase":"MEDIAN","annotation":"Odd count(3): median=lower.peek()=2. Invariant: lower.size >= upper.size, and every element in lower ≤ every element in upper."},{"line":16,"lineCode":"return (lower.peek() + upper.peek()) / 2.0;","variables":{"lower.peek":2,"upper.peek":3,"median":2.5},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"After adding 4th element: even count, median=(lower.max+upper.min)/2."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["Lower Max-Heap"] --> B["Holds smaller half"]
    C["Upper Min-Heap"] --> D["Holds larger half"]
    E["Invariant: lower.size >= upper.size\nAND lower.max <= upper.min"] --> F["Median = lower.max (odd) or avg (even)"]
    B --> E
    D --> E
    G["addNum: always balance to maintain invariant"]
    style F fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
CLASS MedianFinder:
  lower = MaxHeap()  // smaller half
  upper = MinHeap()  // larger half

  addNum(num):
    lower.add(num)             // always add to lower first
    upper.add(lower.poll())    // move lower's max to upper
    IF upper.size > lower.size:  // rebalance: lower should be >= upper
      lower.add(upper.poll())

  findMedian():
    IF lower.size > upper.size: RETURN lower.peek()
    RETURN (lower.peek() + upper.peek()) / 2.0
examples.explanation: The invariant: all elements in lower (max-heap) ≤ all elements in upper (min-heap). lower.size = upper.size or upper.size+1. The median is always accessible at the tops of these heaps. Add: always add to lower (via upper to maintain the cross-heap ordering invariant). The two-step add (lower→upper→maybe back) keeps the invariant. findMedian is O(1), addNum is O(log n).
examples.realWorldUse: Real-time median of network latency. Financial rolling median (price streams). Sensor data analysis (continuous median without sorting all data). The two-heap pattern for median generalizes to: any split at a percentile (e.g., 75th percentile: lower holds 75%, upper holds 25%). Signal processing. Medical monitoring.

examples.title: Task Scheduler — Greedy with Max-Heap
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"freq array + max-heap","variables":{"tasks":"AAABBC","n":2,"freq":"A=3,B=2,C=1","maxHeap":"[3,2,1]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Tasks: A(3), B(2), C(1). n=2 means 2 cooldown slots between same task. Max-heap by frequency."},{"line":13,"lineCode":"for (int i = 0; i <= n; i++)","variables":{"cycle":1,"temp":"[]","time":0},"phase":"CYCLE","annotation":"Cycle 1 (length n+1=3): Poll A(3→2), poll B(2→1), poll C(1→0). time=3. temp=[2,1,0]."},{"line":17,"lineCode":"for (int f : temp) if (f > 0) maxHeap.offer(f);","variables":{"maxHeap":"[2,1]","time":3},"phase":"REQUEUE","annotation":"Re-add A(2) and B(1). C(0) done. Heap: [2,1]."},{"line":13,"lineCode":"Cycle 2","variables":{"cycle":2,"time":6},"phase":"CYCLE","annotation":"Cycle 2: poll A(2→1), poll B(1→0), idle slot. time=6."},{"line":13,"lineCode":"Cycle 3","variables":{"cycle":3,"time":7},"phase":"CYCLE","annotation":"Cycle 3: poll A(1→0). Only 1 task left, no idle needed. time=7. Return 7."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["tasks=AAABBC n=2"] --> B["Heap: [A=3,B=2,C=1]"]
    B --> C["Cycle 1: A,B,C → time=3"]
    C --> D["Heap: [A=2,B=1]"]
    D --> E["Cycle 2: A,B,idle → time=6"]
    E --> F["Heap: [A=1]"]
    F --> G["Cycle 3: A → time=7"]
    G --> H["Done: 7 intervals ✓"]
    style H fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION leastInterval(tasks, n):
  count frequencies → maxHeap (by frequency)
  time = 0

  WHILE maxHeap not empty:
    temp = []
    // Execute one cycle of n+1 slots
    FOR i = 0 to n:
      IF heap not empty: temp.add(heap.poll() - 1)
      time++
      IF heap empty AND all temp == 0: BREAK

    // Re-add tasks that still have remaining count
    FOR freq in temp: IF freq > 0: heap.add(freq)

  RETURN time
examples.explanation: Greedy: always execute the most frequent remaining task to minimize idle time. Process in cycles of n+1 (one per task type + cooldown). Each cycle: poll the n+1 most frequent tasks, execute them, re-enqueue with decremented count. If fewer than n+1 tasks are available, idle slots fill the cycle. The heap ensures we always pick the globally most needed task. O(total_time × n) ≈ O(n × |tasks|).
examples.realWorldUse: CPU scheduling with cooldown (same process needs minimum gap). Rate limiting (same API endpoint needs cooldown). Assembly line sequencing (same machine needs setup time between uses). Database connection pool management. Job scheduling in distributed systems with resource locks.

examples.title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path — Priority Queue
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"Arrays.fill(dist, MAX_VALUE); dist[src]=0;","variables":{"src":0,"dist":"[0,∞,∞,∞]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Graph: 4 nodes. dist[0]=0, all others ∞. Heap: [(0,0)]."},{"line":13,"lineCode":"int[] curr = heap.poll();","variables":{"d":0,"u":0,"heap":"[(0,0)]"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Poll (d=0, u=0). dist[0]=0 == d=0 → not stale. Relax neighbors of node 0."},{"line":18,"lineCode":"if (dist[u] + w < dist[v])","variables":{"u":0,"neighbors":"[(1,4),(2,1)]","dist":"[0,4,1,∞]"},"phase":"RELAX","annotation":"Relax: dist[1]=min(∞, 0+4)=4. dist[2]=min(∞, 0+1)=1. Push (4,1) and (1,2)."},{"line":14,"lineCode":"if (d > dist[u]) continue;","variables":{"d":1,"u":2,"dist[2]":1},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Poll (1,2). d=1==dist[2]=1 → valid. Relax node 2's neighbors."},{"line":18,"lineCode":"relaxing from node 2","variables":{"dist":"[0,3,1,7]"},"phase":"RELAX","annotation":"dist[1]=min(4, 1+2)=3! Update. dist[3]=min(∞,1+6)=7. Push (3,1),(7,3)."},{"line":1,"lineCode":"done","variables":{"dist":"[0,3,1,7]"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Final shortest distances from node 0: [0,3,1,7]. O((V+E) log V) time."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["Start: dist=[0,∞,∞,∞] heap=[(0,0)]"] --> B["Poll (0,0): relax neighbors"]
    B --> C["dist=[0,4,1,∞] heap=[(1,2),(4,1)]"]
    C --> D["Poll (1,2): relax neighbors"]
    D --> E["dist=[0,3,1,7] heap=[(3,1),(4,1)stale,(7,3)]"]
    E --> F["Poll (3,1): relax"]
    F --> G["Poll (4,1): d>dist[1] → SKIP (stale)"]
    G --> H["dist=[0,3,1,7] ✓"]
    style H fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION dijkstra(graph, src):
  dist = array of ∞, dist[src]=0
  heap = MinHeap by distance
  heap.add((0, src))

  WHILE heap not empty:
    (d, u) = heap.poll()
    IF d > dist[u]: CONTINUE  // stale — better path already found

    FOR each (v, weight) in graph[u]:
      IF dist[u] + weight < dist[v]:
        dist[v] = dist[u] + weight
        heap.add((dist[v], v))

  RETURN dist
examples.explanation: Dijkstra: always process the unvisited node with smallest known distance (greedy). Priority queue enables O(log V) extraction of minimum. Stale entries: when we find a better path to v, we push a new entry but don't remove the old one. The 'if d > dist[u]: continue' check skips outdated entries efficiently. O((V+E) log V) with binary heap. Only works with non-negative edge weights.
examples.realWorldUse: Google Maps / GPS navigation (shortest path). Network routing protocols (OSPF uses Dijkstra). Flight route optimization. Social network minimum hops (weighted version). Game pathfinding (A* = Dijkstra + heuristic). Internet packet routing. The pattern extends to: Prim's MST (same structure, different comparison), A* search (add heuristic to cost).

problems.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
problems.description:
Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element.

**Example:**
Input: nums = [3,2,1,5,6,4], k = 2 → Output: 5
Input: nums = [3,2,3,1,2,4,5,5,6], k = 4 → Output: 4
problems.hint1: Sorting gives O(n log n). Can you do O(n log k) using a min-heap of size k?
problems.hint2: Maintain a min-heap of size k. For each number: add it; if size exceeds k, remove the minimum. What remains?
problems.hint3: After processing all numbers, the heap has the k largest elements. The root (minimum of these k) is the kth largest.
problems.editorial: Min-heap of size k: O(n log k) time, O(k) space. Alternative: QuickSelect O(n) average — partition around pivot, recurse only on the side containing the kth element. Mention QuickSelect in interviews for O(n) average.

problems.title: Top K Frequent Elements
problems.description:
Given an integer array nums and integer k, return the k most frequent elements.

**Example:**
Input: nums = [1,1,1,2,2,3], k = 2 → Output: [1,2]
Input: nums = [1], k = 1 → Output: [1]
problems.hint1: First, count frequencies. Then find the k most frequent — this is a Top-K problem.
problems.hint2: Use a HashMap for frequency count, then a min-heap of size k where priority is frequency. Or use bucket sort on frequencies.
problems.hint3: Min-heap approach: offer (frequency, element) pairs; evict if size > k. Bucket sort: create array of size n+1 where index = frequency, collect buckets from high to low.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ nums.length ≤ 10^5 | -10^4 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^4 | k is in range [1, number of unique elements]
problems.editorial: Two-phase: count frequencies O(n), then Top-K by frequency O(n log k). Bucket sort alternative achieves O(n) overall: create buckets[frequency] = [elements], iterate from highest frequency downward.

problems.title: Find Median from Data Stream
problems.description:
Design a data structure that continuously finds the median of a stream of integers.

- addNum(int num): add an integer to the data structure
- findMedian(): return the median of all elements so far

**Example:**
addNum(1), addNum(2), findMedian() → 1.5, addNum(3), findMedian() → 2
problems.hint1: Maintaining a sorted list and updating median in O(1) requires knowing the middle. How can you always know the middle without sorting?
problems.hint2: Use two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half. The median is determined by the tops of these heaps.
problems.hint3: Invariant: maxHeap.size() >= minHeap.size() and maxHeap.size() - minHeap.size() <= 1. After each insert, rebalance. Median: if sizes equal → avg of both tops; else → top of maxHeap.
problems.constraints: -10^5 ≤ num ≤ 10^5 | At least one element before findMedian() is called | At most 5 * 10^4 calls
problems.editorial: Two-heap pattern: lower (max-heap) and upper (min-heap). Always push to lower first, then move its max to upper to ensure upper's min >= lower's max. Rebalance so lower has same or one more element than upper. O(log n) addNum, O(1) findMedian.

problems.title: Merge K Sorted Lists
problems.description:
Merge k sorted linked lists and return it as one sorted list.

**Example:**
Input: [[1,4,5],[1,3,4],[2,6]]
Output: [1,1,2,3,4,4,5,6]
problems.hint1: You have k sorted lists. The next element in the merged list is the minimum across all current heads. How to find this minimum efficiently?
problems.hint2: Use a min-heap seeded with the head of each non-empty list. Always poll the minimum, add it to the result, and push the next element from the same list.
problems.hint3: Store [value, list-index, element-index] in the heap. After polling [v, li, ei]: add lists[li][ei+1] to the heap if it exists.
problems.editorial: Min-heap of size k: always contains the current front of each non-empty list. O(n log k) time where n = total nodes. Each node is pushed and popped from the heap once. O(k) space for the heap. Compare with divide-and-conquer merge: same O(n log k) but different constant.

problems.title: Task Scheduler
problems.description:
Given a list of tasks (represented by letters) and a cooldown n (same task must have n intervals of gap), return the minimum number of intervals needed to complete all tasks.

**Example:**
Input: tasks = ['A','A','A','B','B','B'], n = 2
Output: 8 → A→B→idle→A→B→idle→A→B
problems.hint1: The bottleneck is the most frequent task. How many idle slots does the most frequent task force?
problems.hint2: Formula: result = max(tasks.length, (maxFreq - 1) * (n + 1) + countOfMaxFreq). This handles both idle-heavy and task-heavy cases.
problems.hint3: Greedy simulation with max-heap: at each step, schedule up to n+1 tasks (the most frequent available), add idle if fewer than n+1 tasks available.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ tasks.length ≤ 10^4 | tasks[i] is uppercase English letter | 0 ≤ n ≤ 100
problems.editorial: Mathematical formula: (maxFreq-1)*(n+1) + countOfMaxFreq is the minimum slots if tasks.length is small. But if tasks.length is larger (many diverse tasks fill all idle slots), the answer is just tasks.length. max() handles both cases. O(n) time, O(1) space.

problems.title: K Closest Points to Origin
problems.description:
Given an array of points on a 2D plane, find the k closest points to the origin (0,0). Distance is Euclidean. Return in any order.

**Example:**
points=[[1,3],[-2,2]], k=1 → Output: [[-2,2]] (distance √8 < √10)
points=[[3,3],[5,-1],[-2,4]], k=2 → Output: [[3,3],[-2,4]]
problems.hint1: Top-K problem — but instead of largest, you need K smallest distances. What kind of heap keeps track of K smallest?
problems.hint2: Use a MAX-heap of size K. For each point, add it; if heap size > K, remove the farthest (max) point. Remaining K points are the closest.
problems.hint3: Compare by squared distance (avoid sqrt for performance): x² + y². Store [dist, x, y] in the max-heap.
problems.editorial: Max-heap of size K: O(n log K). The heap's maximum is always the farthest of the K candidates — evict it when a closer point arrives. QuickSelect gives O(n) average. Use squared distance to avoid floating point.

problems.title: Ugly Number II
problems.description:
An ugly number's prime factors are only 2, 3, and 5. Given integer n, return the nth ugly number. 1 is considered ugly.

**Example:**
n=10 → Output: 12 (sequence: 1,2,3,4,5,6,8,9,10,12)
problems.hint1: The next ugly number is always a multiple of 2, 3, or 5 applied to a previous ugly number. How do you generate them in order?
problems.hint2: Use a min-heap. Start with 1. Each time you extract the minimum, add its multiples by 2, 3, and 5. Use a set to avoid duplicates.
problems.hint3: Or use 3-pointer DP: maintain indices i2, i3, i5 pointing to the ugly number each factor should multiply next. Next ugly = min of ugly[i2]*2, ugly[i3]*3, ugly[i5]*5. Advance the pointer(s) that produced the minimum.
problems.editorial: 3-pointer DP is O(n) time and space — more efficient than O(n log n) heap approach. Advance ALL pointers that produced the minimum (handles duplicates like 6 = 2×3 = 3×2). Same pattern extends to Super Ugly Number (arbitrary prime factors).

problems.title: Reorganize String
problems.description:
Given a string s, rearrange its characters so that no two adjacent characters are the same. Return any valid arrangement, or '' if impossible.

**Example:**
Input: 'aab' → Output: 'aba'
Input: 'aaab' → Output: '' (impossible)
problems.hint1: Greedy: always place the most frequent remaining character. What if the most frequent is the same as the last placed?
problems.hint2: Use a max-heap by frequency. At each step: pop the most frequent, place it. Then pop the second most frequent to place next (avoiding repeat). Re-add both with decremented counts.
problems.hint3: Impossible if max_frequency > ceil(n/2). The greedy interleaving always works if this condition holds.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 500 | s consists of lowercase English letters
problems.editorial: Greedy with max-heap: always alternate between top two most frequent characters. Impossible if any char count > ceil(n/2). The interleaving of top-2 always produces a valid result when possible. O(n log 26) = O(n log k) time.

problems.title: IPO (Initial Public Offering)
problems.description:
Given k projects to complete before an IPO, each with a profit and capital requirement, maximize total capital. Start with initial capital w. Select at most k projects to maximize final capital.

**Example:**
k=2, w=0, profits=[1,2,3], capital=[0,1,1]
Output: 4 (take project 0: w=1, take project 2: w=4)
problems.hint1: At each step you should pick the project with maximum profit among all affordable ones. How do you efficiently find all projects you can currently afford?
problems.hint2: Sort projects by capital requirement. Use a min-heap on capital to unlock projects as w grows, and a max-heap on profit to always pick the best available.
problems.hint3: Repeat k times: add all projects with capital[i] ≤ w to the max-heap (profit). Pick the max-profit project (pop from max-heap), add to w.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ k ≤ 10^5 | 0 ≤ w ≤ 10^9 | n == profits.length == capital.length | 1 ≤ n ≤ 10^5 | 0 ≤ profits[i], capital[i] ≤ 10^4
problems.editorial: Two-heap pattern: min-heap (by capital) acts as a 'lock' — unlock projects as capital grows. Max-heap (by profit) gives the best available project in O(log n). Greedy: always take the highest-profit affordable project. O(n log n) sort + O(k log n) for k rounds.

problems.title: Employee Free Time
problems.description:
Given a list of employees each with a sorted schedule (list of intervals), find the intervals representing the free time for all employees (time not covered by any employee's schedule).

**Example:**
schedule = [[[1,3],[6,7]],[[2,4]],[[2,5],[9,12]]]
Output: [[5,6],[7,9]]
problems.hint1: This is an interval merging problem but across multiple sorted lists. How do you process intervals in chronological order?
problems.hint2: Flatten all intervals into one list and sort by start time. Then merge overlapping intervals. Gaps between merged intervals = free time.
problems.hint3: Or use a min-heap: always process the earliest-starting interval. After merging, the gap before the next interval is free time.
problems.editorial: Flatten + sort approach: O(total_intervals * log(total_intervals)). Heap approach: min-heap of (interval.start, employee_idx, interval_idx) — O(total_intervals * log(employees)). The gap detection logic: if next.start > current_max_end, there's a free window.
